title: From Python to R and back….!
text:
Before I start, I wanted to test the combination of Python and R in the same .qmd file.
Apparently, it’s very simple — just add library(reticulate) in an R cell and you’re all set.
To pass objects (e.g., variables, data frames, and so on…) between the two languages, you need to reference the variable as r.[variable] in Python or py$[variable] in R.
So where do the problems start? When you try to insert Python inline code inside markdown text.
In short, even though your .qmd document contains both R and Python executable code cells, it can execute only R inline code.\ To bypass this, there are a bunch of reticulate:: functions that can help. The two main ones are:

reticulate::py_str() — roughly equivalent to print(str(...))
reticulate::py_eval() — executes a Python expression that returns numbers, strings, lists, or dicts (can be problematic with Python objects like type, classes, or modules)

Example: {r} reticulate::py_eval("str(type(x))") — returns the type of variable x as a string
crumbs: From Python to R and back....!

title: From Python to R and back….!
section: Passing variable from Python to R
text:
Passing variable from Python to R


Code
# <Py>

# Create Python variable py_x
py_x = 10
print(py_x)


10


Type of py_x: <class ‘int’>


Code
# <R>

# Passing Python variable py_x to R 
r_x <- py$py_x
print(r_x)


[1] 10


Class of r_x: integer
crumbs: From Python to R and back....!

title: From Python to R and back….!
section: Passing variable from R to Python
text:
Passing variable from R to Python


Code
# <R>

# Create R variable r_y 
r_y <- 20
print(r_y)


[1] 20


Class of r_y: numeric


Code
# <Py>

# Passing the R variable r_y to Python 
py_y = r.r_y
print(py_y)


20.0


Type of py_y: <class ‘float’>
crumbs: From Python to R and back....!

title: From Python to R and back….!
section: Passing DataFrame from Python to R
text:
Passing DataFrame from Python to R


Code
# <Py>

# Create a pandas df from .csv file
import pandas as pd

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mwaskom/seaborn-data/refs/heads/master/flights.csv"

py_flights = pd.read_csv(url)
py_flights.head()


   year     month  passengers
0  1949   January         112
1  1949  February         118
2  1949     March         132
3  1949     April         129
4  1949       May         121


Type of py_flights: <class ‘pandas.core.frame.DataFrame’>


Code
# <R>

# Passing the pandas df to R data.frame
r_flights <- py$py_flights
head(r_flights)


  year    month passengers
1 1949  January        112
2 1949 February        118
3 1949    March        132
4 1949    April        129
5 1949      May        121
6 1949     June        135


Class of r_flights: data.frame
crumbs: From Python to R and back....!

title: Mapping a City’s Road Network with OpenStreetMap
text:
The goal of this project is to extract, clean, and visualize the road network of Hod Hasharon using OpenStreetMap data, while demonstrating a basic spatial workflow and basic Python tools like OSMnx, geopandas and matplotlib.
Hod Hasharon is a typical suburban city, with population of over 65,000. Most of the population works outside the city, which creates heavy traffic on the outer axis routes, especially during morning hours.


GeoPandas for a spatial analysis.
OSMnx is a Python package to easily download, model, analyze, and visualize street networks and other geospatial features from OpenStreetMap.



This basic workflow include:

Download spatial data from OpenStreetMap using OSMnx
Basic GeoDataFrame inspection and cleaning
CRS transformation and why it matters
Simple spatial analysis (length by road type)
Basic static and interactive visualization
crumbs: Mapping a City's Road Network with OpenStreetMap

title: Mapping a City’s Road Network with OpenStreetMap
text:

This basic workflow include:

title: Mapping a City’s Road Network with OpenStreetMap
text:
Define the place / boundaries
First, we’ll get the city boundaries using the ox.geocode_to_gdf() function. This has 2 main roles:

Make sure that OSM recognizes the place’s name.
Create a polygon of the place’s border which we can use later



Code
city_name = "Hod Hasharon, Israel"

boundary = ox.geocode_to_gdf(city_name)
boundary.head()

Code
# Confirm CRS:
boundary.crs


<Geographic 2D CRS: EPSG:4326>
Name: WGS 84
Axis Info [ellipsoidal]:
- Lat[north]: Geodetic latitude (degree)
- Lon[east]: Geodetic longitude (degree)

- name: World.

boundary Basic plot:

# Basic plot
boundary.plot()
crumbs: Mapping a City's Road Network with OpenStreetMap

title: Mapping a City’s Road Network with OpenStreetMap
text:
Load the roads dataset






Note



In this section we’ll load the full dataset and clean the data after loading, using geopandas


Now we can load the roads and paths. First we need to define the tags.
tags is the key:value of the map feature we want to load. full list of the tags can be found here.
We’ll use 'road_type': True to retrieve the whole roads, paths and cycle tracks and other recognised routes.
OSMnx has a few features_from_*() methods to load data, in which we’ll focus on 2 of them.

features_from_place()
This method uses the place name we confirmed earlier, it’s simpler method but depends on OSM’s place definitions:


Code
# Defining 'tags' (the value can be a list of specific features)
tags = {'highway': True}

# Using features_from_place()
city_roads = ox.features_from_place(city_name, tags=tags)


We got 2008 features with 85 attributes


features_from_polygon()
In this method we use the polygon object we created, which gives more control and especially useful with custom boundaries:


Code
# Using features_from_polygon()
city_roads_from_polygon = ox.features_from_polygon(boundary.geometry.iloc[0],
                                           tags=tags)








Note



The polygon argument is boundary.geometry.iloc[0] which is shapely.geometry.polygon.Polygon type object


Here we also got 2008 features with 85 attributes
crumbs: Mapping a City's Road Network with OpenStreetMap

title: Mapping a City’s Road Network with OpenStreetMap
text:
Filtering geometry types
Let’s check the geometry type we got, and filter out all non-LineString features


Code
city_roads.geometry.geom_type.value_counts()


LineString    1776
Point          227
Polygon          5
Name: count, dtype: int64




Code
city_roads_linestrings = city_roads[
    city_roads.geometry.geom_type.isin(["LineString", "MultiLineString"])]








Tip



Although there aren’t any MultiLineString geometric types features in this gdf, it’s a good practice to include them.


After filtering, the gdf contains 1776 features
crumbs: Mapping a City's Road Network with OpenStreetMap

title: Mapping a City’s Road Network with OpenStreetMap
text:
Cleaning columns
The necessary columns are:

‘geometry’ - IMPORTANT!
‘highway’ - The road type according to the OSM’s key:value definition (will be changed to road_type)
‘name’ - Streets name (if available)



Code
city_roads_linestrings = city_roads_linestrings[['geometry', 'highway', 'name']]
city_roads_linestrings.rename({"highway": "road_type"}, axis="columns", inplace=True) # Rename column


The clean gdf contains 1776 features and 3 attributes
crumbs: Mapping a City's Road Network with OpenStreetMap

title: Mapping a City’s Road Network with OpenStreetMap
text:
Reproject the CRS
Before we add the length of the segments, we have to check the gdf’s CRS to see if it has meaningful units of measurement:


Code
# Check gdf CRS 
city_roads_linestrings.crs


<Geographic 2D CRS: EPSG:4326>
Name: WGS 84
Axis Info [ellipsoidal]:
- Lat[north]: Geodetic latitude (degree)
- Lon[east]: Geodetic longitude (degree)
Area of Use:
- name: World.
- bounds: (-180.0, -90.0, 180.0, 90.0)
Datum: World Geodetic System 1984 ensemble
- Ellipsoid: WGS 84
- Prime Meridian: Greenwich




Code
city_roads_linestrings.geometry.length.head()


element  id      
way      31661888    0.008288
         31664220    0.004763
         31664319    0.001693
         31664320    0.001673
         31664581    0.003211
dtype: float64


In WGS84 (EPSG: 4326) coordinates and measurements are in degrees, which make distance values hard to understand and not very practical on small areas. To make measurements meaningful we need to reproject the spatial dataset to a projected CRS (ex. UTM) where units are in meters.






Tip



For a full list of all the available CRSs, check epsg.io site




Code
city_roads_UTM = city_roads_linestrings.to_crs(epsg=32636)


So now, if we check the CRS we’ll get:


Code
city_roads_UTM.crs


<Projected CRS: EPSG:32636>
Name: WGS 84 / UTM zone 36N
Axis Info [cartesian]:
- E[east]: Easting (metre)
- N[north]: Northing (metre)
Area of Use:
- name: Between 30°E and 36°E, northern hemisphere between equator and 84°N, onshore and offshore. Belarus. Cyprus. Egypt. Ethiopia. Finland. Israel. Jordan. Kenya. Lebanon. Moldova. Norway. Russian Federation. Saudi Arabia. Sudan. Syria. Türkiye (Turkey). Uganda. Ukraine.
- bounds: (30.0, 0.0, 36.0, 84.0)
Coordinate Operation:
- name: UTM zone 36N
- method: Transverse Mercator
Datum: World Geodetic System 1984 ensemble
- Ellipsoid: WGS 84
- Prime Meridian: Greenwich


And if we check the length of the first few features, we’ll get:


Code
city_roads_UTM.geometry.length.head()


element  id      
way      31661888    781.878730
         31664220    528.026825
         31664319    173.824444
         31664320    168.902683
         31664581    355.949285
dtype: float64
crumbs: Mapping a City's Road Network with OpenStreetMap

title: Mapping a City’s Road Network with OpenStreetMap
section: Calculating the total length of each ‘road_type’ value
text:
Calculating the total length of each ‘road_type’ value


Code
roads_types = pd.DataFrame(city_roads_UTM.groupby('road_type')['length (m)'].sum())
roads_types['percent of total length'] = round((roads_types['length (m)'] / sum(roads_types['length (m)']) * 100), 2)
roads_types.sort_values(by='length (m)', ascending=False)

title: Mapping a City’s Road Network with OpenStreetMap

Advanced action-items
Here’s some advanced action-items which can be made on this project:

Divide the road types into 3 bins - connectivity roads, local roads, walking pathways and bicycle routes
Compare with other cities

title: About
text: About this site

title: Wellcome
text:
This is a Quarto website.
To learn more about Quarto websites visit https://quarto.org/docs/websites.

title: Project Setup Guide
text:
My main goal with this project is to create a blog / web site about spatial analysis, with two main focuses: analysis project where I analyze a specific issue, and tutorial topics where I explain a specific issue (mostly for my future self!).
I will be using Python, R and SQL with duckdb, utilizing Positron with Quarto
Instead of trying to build the whole website at once, I’m using a “staged approach”:

Create some standalone .qmd files in organized subfolders.
Once initial content is ready, implement a Quarto Website structure to glue them together.

title: Project Setup Guide
text:
1. Initialize the Environment
I started by creating an R project folder in Positron with renv for reproducible R dependencies and Git-ready version control. Next, I added a Python environment using UV: {pwsh} uv init

title: Project Setup Guide
text:
2. Core Packages
I installed the primary spatial and data science packages for each language:
Python (uv add):

geopandas, rasterio (Vector/Raster analysis)
duckdb (SQL)
matplotlib (Visualization)
folium, leafmap (Interactive Visualization)
jupyter (Engine support)

R (install.packages):

tidyverse (Data Science packages collection)
sf (Vector analysis)
terra (Raster analysis)
reticulate (The bridge between R and Python)







Note



I am using reticulate for the integration between R and Python environments in the same *.qmd file.

title: Project Setup Guide
text:
3. The environment Bridge
The biggest challenge was ensuring reticulate could find the uv environment when rendering files inside subfolders.
For local work on Positron, I use a .Renviron file in the project root, which is Git-ignored:
# .Renviron (Local only!)
RETICULATE_PYTHON = "../.venv/Scripts/python.exe"






Note



In this manner, the .qmd files need to be inside a one-level sub-folder and not directly in the project root. For more flexibility, a full path should be provided

title: Project Setup Guide
text:
4. Folder Structure
The repository is organized into two main content folders:

projects/: Analysis projects
tutorials/: General guides

And now our repository is ready!

title: Project Setup Guide
text:
1. Implement a Quarto Website Project






Tip



Quarto has good site with guides and explanations about its files and projects format


After creating the first few .qmd files, it’s time to implement the Website Project. First I created a separate ‘test’ folder for the Quarto Website Project. That gave me the chance to “play” and test the features with this format in a clean and safe environment.
After familiarizing myself with the Project’s format, I copied the relevant files: _quarto.yml, index.qmd, about.qmd and styles.css to the repository root.
In _quarto.yml file added the line:
project:
  output-dir: docs
Doing this, make deployment of the page through GitHub Pages, much easier.

title: Project Setup Guide
text:
2. Publish in GitHub
Once the site is updated, the necessary steps are:

{pwsh} quarto render
{pwsh} git add .
commit
push
